text: The surgeon begins the robotic rectopexy procedure for full thickness rectal prolapse by suspending the uterus to clear the surgical field. The camera is positioned in port three, with two robotic arms on the left and one on the right.

The surgeon begins a medial dissection over the sacral promontory to enter the retro-rectal space. The nerves are clearly visible and are preserved by sweeping them posteriorly off the posterior plane of the mesorectum. The surgeon performs a full posterior mobilization, right lateral mobilization, and anterior mobilization, while leaving the left side untouched. The camera is repositioned to arm two for a better angle, now positioned more towards the midline, as the dissection continues.

The surgeon visualizes the deep anterior cul-de-sac and begins defining the dissection plane in the rectovaginal septum, carefully avoiding injury to the vagina and rectum. As the dissection progresses distally, the surgeon refrains from using cautery and performs a digital rectal exam upon reaching the perineal body, indicating the limit of the dissection. The right-sided dissection is then completed.
The surgeon navigates the TME plane, closely adjacent to the rectal wall on the elevator floor, aiming for deep mobilization. The enococcygeal ligament is visible during this part of the dissection.

The surgeon ports back to arm number three to place pexy sutures. Bipolar cautery is applied over the bone to control bleeding before stitching. The nerve is pushed aside, and arm number one pulls the right lateral stalk into position. A 2-0 silk suture is used to take a bite of the periosteum and the lateral stalk, securing it to the rectal wall. An additional parallel bite is taken for extra security.

The surgeon ties down the second pexy suture, ensuring the rectum is taut and on tension. The suture passes through the periosteum, up through the lateral stalk, and to the rectal wall before being tied down. The peritoneal opening in the right lateral rectal dissection is then closed using a V-lock suture.

The surgeon ensures the ureter is identified before completing the suturing. The rectum is visibly on tension with the pexy suture, and the surgeon checks for any redundancy, folding, or kinking. The uterus is lowered, and bleeding from the suture is addressed before concluding the procedure.
